quotedTweet.text:
Looking at the interview, it seems yesterday's piece needs some revision.

Yesterday I wrote about the venue of the prospective agreement, the mechanism meant to enforce it, and the actors who will not sign it. Then I came across an SNN interview in which Mahmoud Nabavian, deputy chairman of the Iranian Parliament's National Security Commission, read out the original text directly and launched a frontal attack on the negotiating team.

Nabavian is a hardliner opposed to the negotiations themselves, so his testimony carries its own bias. But on the strength of the fact that he was quoting the wording — and on the basis of the critique now coming from the hardline camp — yesterday's piece needs correcting.

First, the parts where my earlier analysis pointed in a similar direction.

What Held Up

1. The diagnosis of "a document that seals the disputes inside a 60-day window"

The starting point of my analysis was that this MOU is not a document that resolved the core disputes — nuclear, control, money — but one that repackaged them, deferred to "negotiations 60 days later," so that only the signing becomes possible. Nabavian's central objection takes aim at exactly this structure. He criticized the fact that all the major benefits accruing to Iran — $300 billion in reconstruction, an end to sanctions, resolution of the nuclear issue, US troop withdrawal — are bound up in a "final agreement" whose date is unclear and indefinitely extendable.

2. Hormuz — the "rebranding of control"

I had argued that what Iran gave up at Hormuz was only the word "toll," while the economic substance (service fees) and the operational substance (control) remained alive — and I forecast that "a reappraisal will come once the details of an Iranian-managed reopening are made public." This part turned out more dramatic than I expected. According to Nabavian's reading, from the hardline vantage point that control itself has evaporated from the text. He revealed that the earlier version's explicit name "Strait of Hormuz" had been generalized to "commercial ships from the Persian Gulf to the Sea of Oman," and that the US had later inserted the word "unlimited." His claim: "arrangements" means not Iranian management but merely preparation for reopening, and nowhere in the text is Iranian control of the strait preserved. In other words, my initial read — "the claim to control is alive" — exists from the negotiating team's standpoint, but on the wording of the text itself it is exposed to the hardliners' rebuttal.

3. UNSC endorsement is not a guarantee

In the earlier piece I concluded with "the lesson of Resolution 2231 — institutionalization only raises the cost of exit; it does not prevent exit." Nabavian's critique of Clause 14 says the same thing in an insider's language: "A binding UNSC resolution is not a real guarantee. We already lived through this with the JCPOA and 2231 — the US withdrew, yet Iran alone was left trapped in international obligations, and only snapback became possible." He goes a step further: "Once the agreement goes to the Security Council, Iran does not control the text, and the US and other members slip in additional restrictions — missiles, arms, inspections (precisely the 2231 case)." Strong corroboration that my initial inference is being raised, identically, inside the Iranian parliament.

4. The US domestic bottleneck — Congress, OFAC, banks

Near the end of the piece I added the asymmetry that "Congress cannot guarantee non-aggression, but it can block sanctions relief." Nabavian confirms it outright: "The US cannot by itself cancel UNSC or IAEA resolutions, and many sanctions are congressional statutes that Trump cannot remove on his own." The three-layer bottleneck logic — OFAC, Congress, bank compliance — was being read the same way in Tehran.

5. The inversion of the money sequence

I had flagged the sequence inversion as central: "2013 was performance first, money later; this time it is a demand for upfront payment." Nabavian confirms the structure with more precise figures.

6. The documentation of Lebanon

My earlier piece read the shift from April's "end of war" carve-out to this round's "ceasefire across all fronts, Lebanon included" as deliberate engineering. Nabavian, too, concedes that Clause 1 at least was "improved in Iran's favor compared to the earlier version" — "end of war" was strengthened to "immediate and permanent end of military operations on all fronts, including Lebanon," closing the room for the US to declare the war over while continuing 'defensive' operations.

Where My Initial Read Was Wrong

1. The "lifting is easy" dichotomy — I missed the re-sanctions axis

I split the new Security Council resolution into two parts: lifting the legacy sanctions (easy) and the new enforcement device (hard). That dichotomy dropped one crucial axis. As Nabavian points out — "the text does not prevent the US from imposing new sanctions the very next day, so the sanctions remain 100% effectively in place." Even if a nominal lifting occurs, if re-designation under a different name is possible, the lifting is hollow. What Iran really wants is not "lifting" but a "guarantee against re-sanctioning," and a lifting without that is meaningless to Iran. My earlier analysis, boxed into "lifting vs. enforcement," failed to see the third axis of re-sanctioning. This asymmetry interlocks with the nuclear section, too — Iran repeatedly provides written guarantees that it "will not produce or acquire nuclear weapons," while the US provides no equivalent guarantee on future sanctions.

2. The money figure — "half of $24 billion" was imprecise

Following the Mehr report, I wrote "half of $24 billion paid upfront." Nabavian's text is more precise. Iran's earlier proposal was "$12 billion immediately after signing, the rest within 30 days," and after the US rejected it, the current version weakened to the vague phrase "assets to be made available based on progress in negotiations" — with no definition of "progress" and no figure. In other words, the clear guarantee of "$12 billion immediately" has disappeared. My sequence-inversion thesis held, but the figure, and the direction of the retreat (toward terms even less favorable to Iran), need correcting. And this shows that the "$24 billion" headline making the rounds is not a settled condition but one version within the negotiating process.

3. Nuclear dilution — "domestic blend-down = an Iranian win" was an oversimplification

I framed the gap between "what Trump sold Netanyahu (removal)" and "what the document says (domestic blend-down)" as a US–Israel rift. The frame itself holds, but treating "domestic blend-down" as if it were an Iranian victory was an oversimplification. The actual wording Nabavian read out is "the dilution of all nuclear material on site, at minimum under IAEA supervision and under American considerations." It is domestic dilution — but dilution under American control. Hardliners see even this as "an American demand packaged in Iran's language." My earlier frame is not weakened by this (the US–Israel gap stands), but the nuance that "Iran fended off removal, so it amounts to a win" needs correcting.

4. The "clever Iranian design" frame — it tilted to one side

This is the part needing the largest correction. Taking the documentation of Lebanon as my starting point, I loaded the MOU rather heavily with Iranian strategic agency (deterrence + exit + raising the cost of erosion through multilateralization). Nabavian's whole testimony paints the opposite picture. He reads Hormuz, the nuclear file, the money, the withdrawal — nearly every item — as "American demands presented in Iran's language," i.e., the result of Iran retreating with each successive version. "Every time we submitted it and it changed, we retreated" is his summary.

The truth lies between two biases. I lean toward Iranian agency; Nabavian (as a hardliner) leans toward Iranian capitulation. Netting them out, the MOU is neither "Iran's clever trap" nor "Iran's total surrender," but something closer to a US-led asymmetric bargain in which Iran won one square — Lebanon — and gave ground on Hormuz, the nuclear file, and the money. Lebanon is nearly the only square Iran actively secured; on the rest, it was largely on the defensive. The center of gravity of my earlier piece has, in effect, collapsed.

What I Did Not Address Before, but Must Add After This Interview

1. The paradox of Iran freezing its own leverage

This is the strongest point, absent from my earlier analysis. According to Nabavian, during the waiting period Iran freezes — and proposed to freeze, itself — three things: its current nuclear status (no enrichment), the non-reconstruction of the damaged facilities, and the endurance of US sanctions. On top of that it accepts the continued presence of US forces in the region. In other words, if the final agreement is extended indefinitely, Iran freezes its own cards during the wait while the US keeps its sanctions and its troops in place. His rhetorical question — "Is this really America's proposal, or ours?" — is the crux.

This deepens my reading of the 60-day window by one level. I had seen the window only as "a time bomb where the nuclear and control disputes reignite." But before that, the window is a period in which Iran voluntarily ties down its supply and nuclear leverage. In the short term this is downward pressure on oil (Iran restrains its incentive to provoke), but it is simultaneously a stretch in which domestic hardliner discontent accumulates. The vagueness of the withdrawal clause ("US forces leave the area 'surrounding Iran' 30 days after the final agreement" — timing undefined, "surrounding" distance undefined) amplifies it. Lifting the blockade is nearly the only US-side implementation item with a fixed date; the rest of the US implementation is all linked to the variable "final agreement."

2. A "third generation" of guarantee discourse — the hardliners' automatic-retaliation trigger

I had organized Iran's guarantee model as first generation (congressional ratification) → second generation (multilateral institutions, the UNSC, economic lock-in). Nabavian rejects even that second generation and demands a third: instead of a UNSC resolution, write into the document automatic consequences — that if the US violates the deal, Iran exits the NPT, fully closes the Strait of Hormuz, and resumes enrichment at unlimited levels. In other words, inside Iran the guarantee discourse is reverting from "institution" to "consequence," and the negotiating team's second-generation line is being squeezed by hardliners who call it "a trap, too." Whether this comes to pass, however, remains uncertain.

3. The Iranian domestic bottleneck — symmetry with the Washington bottleneck

My earlier piece dealt only with the US-side bottleneck (Congress, OFAC, banks). Nabavian's public revolt itself shows that even if the negotiating team reaches a signature, friction can arise at Iran's domestic ratification and implementation stage. Because the Hormuz "control" wording and the nuclear "American considerations" wording are the focal targets of the hardliners, an interpretive struggle over these two clauses will erupt inside Iran after signing. A symmetric structure in which both sides' domestic politics gnaw at the agreement simultaneously — this was missing from the earlier analysis.

4. The instability of the text's very authenticity — a meta-level caveat

The most important meta-lesson. Even Nabavian conceded that he is "told that what I have seen is not the final version." An Iranian deputy committee chairman has, in effect, admitted that the reported text I relied on when writing the earlier piece may not be the final one. At the same time, his own reading may be a hardliner's selective citation. No single version should be believed as is.

Yet paradoxically, the very "differences" between those versions may be the most honest signal. Which word was added ("unlimited") and which words were dropped ("Strait of Hormuz," "compensation/reparations," "$12 billion immediately") reveal where the center of gravity of the negotiation is shifting.

It was only because yesterday and today happen to be holidays for me that I could attempt this at all. This exercise has given me no certainty whatsoever. The one thing I was able to confirm is the symbolic weight the MOU carries. Reading the posts that surfaced across various X accounts today, and Nabavian's interview, it is clear that — unlike what gets digested in the headlines — the two sides are negotiating under a thick layer of complexity around a particular documentary form: the wording, the nuance, and even the framework that follows. The US will have its own internal backlash, and Iran will have the same. This is precisely why I bought the Z26 calls: I wanted to step outside the optimism and the pessimism about the negotiations, and to place a bet closer to physics and mathematics — one with the higher probability. My efforts yesterday and today may, in truth, have been an absurd waste of time. Have a good weekend.

Everyone on the wire is talking about the US government cracking down on Anthropic. 

But didn't Amodei literally ask for this with his nonstop doomer marketing? 

He was the one preaching AI needs heavy regulation and goverment should have the teeth to shut down models if necessary. 

They’re getting exactly what he engineered, so why is he throwing a tantrum on the tape, crying about why OpenAI gets a free pass while Anthropic takes the full hit? 

Asking for a friend.

Thank you Mr. President, I love u! You actually pulled the trigger. 

Since my entire book is sitting purely in long dated calls anyway, I’m absolutely applauding the move.

Taking a massive L and actually owning up to it is no easy feat. Of course there are plenty more Ls coming down the pipe. Let’s be real, no idiot on the street actually believes this setup is going to work.

Now I can finally tune out the garbage headlines on the wire and get right back to the math. As long as Barak Ravid is the one single-handedly driving the oil complex, oil stands zero chance of rallying in the face of back to back VaR shocks.

Big thanks again Mr. President. Have a killer weekend everyone!

COT on #FX in week to 9 June was dominated by broad and aggressive dollar buying after a stronger-than-expected US jobs report on 5 June helped the greenback post its strongest daily gain in more than two months. By the end of the reporting week, the dollar had risen around 0.7% against a basket of major currencies.
The one-sided focus on dollar strength triggered a 69% surge in the aggregate net long dollar position against the eight IMM currency futures, lifting it to USD 28 billion, the highest level in fourteen months.
Selling was broad-based and led by the euro, where the net long was reduced by 34.9k contracts to 14k contracts, equivalent to around USD 2 billion. This was followed by a reduction in bullish bets on the Australian dollar, and increased short positioning in Canadian dollar, Australian dollar, Japanese yen and British pound.
As a result, the speculative net short in the Japanese yen climbed to a fresh 23-month high of 146k contracts, equivalent to roughly USD 11.5 billion. It remains the largest short position held against the dollar despite repeated intervention warnings from Japanese authorities.
COT on #forex breakdown in the non-commercial positioning https://t.co/WOXFVHe3rK

Trump declared the Iran war over this morning. Bitcoin jumped 3.4%. He has said this before. Is this time different? 

Bitcoin opened at $63,553 this morning, up 3.4% after Trump said the war in Iran has ended and a deal is near. Oil dropped. Brent crude fell toward the mid-$80s. Risk assets rallied. 

The problem is that Trump has tweeted this dozens of times. Has he discovered the hack to make markets go up forever? 

Now all eyes are on the Fed meeting.

US SPR Drawdown & Looming Crude Shortage ! 

The attacks on the Indian seamen aboard shadow fleet were an American sign of desperation creeping. Trump is escalating to de-escalate be it US Navy strikes in Gulf Of Oman or IDF strikes in Beirut ! 

The clocks of crude drawn down in SPR in USA & the cushing in Oklahoma are running close to emergency levels which is going to make it difficult for Americans to manage crude prices. They are soon going to be hitting emergency levels. 

Thus you see this escalation to pile up pressure on Iranians to yield for an agreement. US Navy’s demonstrative strike on Shadow oil fleet tankers simply happens to be its cold ruthless strategy to enforce the blockade and raise the escalation level up. 

And very unfortunately Indian crews operating in these Iranian shadow oil trade tankers have caught up in this bid for escalation for President Trump. Consider this data below separating fact from narrative to understand the severity of shock Oil market is staring at.

As a vital chokepoint, Hormuz normally handles 25% of global seaborne oil trade (roughly 20% of total global consumption). This disruption has choked off an estimated 11 to 16 million barrels of daily supply, with tanker traffic plummeting to a mere 10% of pre-conflict levels.

To counteract this massive supply shock, global oil stockpiles are currently draining at a highly unsustainable rate of 4.8 million barrels per day. 

To stabilize markets, the International Energy Agency (IEA) initiated its largest emergency intervention in history, orchestrating a coordinated 400-million-barrel release across 32 allied nations. 

The United States is shouldering the largest burden of this global effort, authorizing a massive 172-million-barrel drawdown from its Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR).

This aggressive release drops the SPR from 415 million barrels down to 243 million barrels. The reserve now sits at a precarious 34% of its 714-million-barrel total capacity, marking its lowest operating level since February 1982.

International markets are flashing red. Asia-Pacific commercial inventories (excluding China) have suffered a staggering plunge of 70 million barrels, and analysts warn OECD stockpiles will soon hit critical operational stress levels. 

This physical drain has violently impacted global financial markets, with West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude prices surging 47% in two weeks, leaping from $67 to nearly $99 per barrel. 

Meanwhile, the Brent crude international benchmark briefly spiked to $120 a barrel before finding temporary stabilization around $100. U.S. commercial oil infrastructure is fracturing under the weight of the Middle East blockade. 

Cushing, Oklahoma the vital delivery hub for U.S. crude is nearing catastrophic operational failure. 

Following an aggressive 11.3-million-barrel plunge between April and June 2026, Cushing’s inventory crashed to just 21.6 million barrels. This alarming figure sits dangerously close to the facility's 20-million-barrel "tank bottoms" operational floor.

Falling below this 26.7% capacity threshold of the 75-million-barrel tank farms renders the system functionally unusable due to physical pumping constraints and sludge accumulation. 

With less than a 2-million-barrel buffer remaining, industry data projects Cushing will hit this floor within one to two weeks if current drawdown rates persist.

Clocks are ticking by, American Oil supplies are soon reach emergency levels in few weeks. And a lot of crude inventory at bottom cannot be used as that is lifeblood to keep the system running. 

We are soon hitting the tipping point on that in commercial crud inventories and even other SPR levels will reach emergency levels. Where US can either prioritise itself or its allies for crude supplies. 

And that is where fuel shock is loading. Every day & every week adds more pressure on USA. Iranians know this and they are playing Trump in a game of nerve wrecking negotiations.

After the United States, Now Britain Takes Major Maritime Action; Claims of 23 Indian Seafarers Aboard Detained Russian Oil Tanker Spark Discussion!

Marine Tanks | International Desk

Amid rising tensions in the Strait of Hormuz and the Gulf of Oman over the past few days, where U.S. and allied military forces have been actively involved in safeguarding commercial shipping, Britain has now carried out a significant maritime operation of its own. British armed forces intercepted and boarded the oil tanker Smyrtos, reportedly linked to Russia’s so-called “shadow fleet,” in the English Channel.

The incident has attracted considerable attention within the Indian maritime community, as claims circulating on social media suggest that the vessel was carrying 23 Indian and 3 Georgian seafarers. However, at the time of publication, neither India’s Ministry of External Affairs (MEA), the Directorate General of Shipping (DGS), nor British authorities had officially confirmed this information.

Following the incident, an audio message has also gone viral on social media. In the recording, purportedly shared by a crew member on board, it is claimed that two helicopters approached the vessel shortly after it exited the English Channel. The entire crew was then reportedly instructed to assemble at a designated location on board.

According to the viral audio, the officials who boarded the vessel first inquired about the number of crew members and the availability of provisions, including food, water, and other essential supplies. It is alleged that the authorities sought to ensure that the seafarers had adequate rations and necessary resources available on board.

The British government has confirmed the operation and described it as an important step against Russia’s sanctions-evasion oil trading network. However, no official details regarding the nationality or exact number of crew members have been released.

Marine Tanks Fact Check

✅ British forces' action against a tanker linked to Russia’s “shadow fleet” has been officially confirmed.
✅ The operation took place in the English Channel.
✅ Britain has officially acknowledged the action.
⚠️ Claims that 23 Indian and 3 Georgian seafarers were on board remain unverified.
⚠️ The viral audio recording has not been independently authenticated.
⚠️ No official statement has been issued regarding the nationality of the crew.

USrael Empire's Death Spiral 26-06-14:

The empire ran its AI on schoolchildren, rattled sabres across half a dozen straits and borders, and still couldn't get Iran to sign anything.

West Asia:

🔻 Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei defended the company's AI after US military systems it powered guided a strike on an elementary school in Minab, Iran, killing 168 people — predominantly young girls — stating the attack did not cross the company's stated "red lines."

🔻 Qatari negotiators flew to Tehran to facilitate a US-Iran memorandum of understanding, but Iranian sources confirmed no final decision has been made, with political, legal, and technical dimensions of the proposed agreement still under review.

🔻 US President Donald Trump and Pakistani officials asserted the MOU would be signed Sunday to reopen the Strait of Hormuz; Iranian Foreign Ministry spokesperson Esmaeil Baghaei explicitly denied a Sunday signing, citing US instability, while hardline demonstrators in Tehran demanded the resignation of Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi over the proposed concessions.

🔻 Satellite imagery confirms that Iran's June 11 retaliatory strike completely destroyed a US AN/TPS-59 long-range early warning radar in Bahrain and demolished two aviation fuel storage tanks at Sheikh Isa Air Base.

🔻 Secretary of State Marco Rubio warned Indian Foreign Minister Subrahmanyam Jaishankar against routing commercial vessels through the Strait of Hormuz, stating all ships must comply with US military instructions and that Iranian oil transport will not be tolerated.

🔻 Hezbollah shot down an Israeli Heron 1 surveillance drone valued at $10 million over the Bekaa Valley; simultaneous ground engagements erupted between Hezbollah fighters and Israeli armoured convoys advancing through Wadi Hassan toward Majdal Zoun in southern Lebanon.

🔻 A 44-year-old Haifa resident was arrested on espionage charges after allegedly furnishing an Iranian agent with photographs of a US Navy pier and port inspection vulnerabilities in exchange for $740 in cryptocurrency.

🔻 Iran's Intelligence Ministry announced the arrest of 126 members of a riot network active during the recent conflict, the dismantling of a four-person Takfiri terrorist cell, and the detention of an operative who attempted to breach sensitive government data centres.

🔻 US military personnel arrived via Chinook helicopters at the Nahb military camp in Yemen's oil-rich Hadhramaut province, with local sources indicating deployment from Al-Rayyan airport in Mukalla or from Arabian Sea naval fleets.

🔻 UK armed forces seized the Cameroonian-flagged tanker Smyrtos in the English Channel, identifying it as part of Russia's shadow fleet; Russian officials accused Prime Minister Keir Starmer of ordering the seizure to divert attention from domestic anti-migrant protests.

🔻 UK Defence Secretary John Healey resigned, citing domestic budget cuts driven by London's ongoing financial and military support for Ukraine — the third minister in the portfolio to step down during the conflict.

🔻 Washington informed European allies it will significantly reduce its NATO military footprint, cutting deployed fighter jets from 150 to 100, withdrawing eight aerial refuelling tankers, and removing an aircraft carrier and submarine from the European theatre.

🔻 President Volodymyr Zelensky signed legislation stripping Russian of its protected status under the European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages.

🔻 Russian military advances near Konstantinovka in the Donetsk region forced Kyiv to begin relocating personnel and industrial equipment from Kramatorsk and Druzhkovka to western Ukraine.

THE SECOND HALF OF 2026 and so on..
OIL, AI, REFLATION, UNDERPRICED CONVEXITY

I have been looking at IV, rho, and funding rates on long-dated $SPX options. (As a reminder, rho measures the sensitivity of the option premium to interest rates).
The steepness of the term structure is surprisingly calm, no kink, albeit elevated. It is no coincidence that I mentioned last year that I'm long ATM straddles. However, there is a significant increase and asymmetry in option premiums, which stems purely from the carry. The funding rate is well above the dividend yield (SOFR 3.60%, Fed Funds 3.50-3.75%).
For the expiries between Dec 17 and Jan 14, I also calculated that the annualized forward drift over this 28-day window comes out to exactly ~6.06%. This is not driven by rate hike fears, but is rather a synthetic usurious rate charged by banks.
The market is explicitly pricing in a slow hiking cycle, one that—both in velocity and magnitude—allows real interest rates to erode further, acting as a hidden QE effect for equities. The market hopes that under political and fiscal constraints, Warsh will be unable to initiate a steeper rate path and will instead, much like Trump, prioritize managing the debt burden (as I pointed out last year). The market sees that the 4.12% bond yield is being eaten alive by reflation, so participants are willing to pay the banks' 4.75% toll just to buy the SPX at a 3.73% earnings yield. This is a painful, reflation-driven melt-up scenario.

☝️What they are failing to account for, however, is that an energy shock by autumn is no longer a mere possibility, but a reality.
The SPR is at 350M barrels, hovering at 1983 lows. Furthermore, the US government is a forced buyer in the future due to the exchange program. The first solicitation is for 86M barrels with an 18–22% in-kind premium, with returns scheduled between Nov 2026 and Sept 2028; the replenishment will bring levels roughly back to the current baseline around July 2028. The reason oil pulled back from $144 to $110, and why inflation hasn't struck yet, is the record-breaking coordinated inventory release. The IEA released 400M+ barrels (301M of which was crude), providing a ~2.5 mb/d buffer for four months—but this is time-limited, as the fixed release volume will rapidly deplete. The weekly SPR drawdown was 9.92M barrels (week of May 15), breaking the 2022 record, and total US crude inventories plunged by ~17.8M barrels in a single week—the largest drop since 1982. Meanwhile, the SPR is approaching its ~240M operational minimum, with a ~70M functional floor.

The market is therefore pricing this as if the shock has passed (because oil pulled back and the release calmed things down), when in reality it is merely buffered, and that buffer will run dry around mid-summer, as I keep saying.
The second wave—price re-acceleration and forced replenishment demand (the government acting as a forced buyer at a +18–22% premium), compounded by the inflationary pass-through—is mechanically hardcoded. It is not hypothetical, and it is completely independent of whether Iran re-escalates or not.
Furthermore, if OECD reserves fall to their lowest levels since 2003 (50 days of forward cover), an exponential rise in energy prices becomes inevitable, forcing an aggressive rate hike. The AI sector is already overleveraged and overbuilt, as I and many others have demonstrated. However, this shock will force a reality check, which they are currently trying to suppress through headline and narrative control.

- Why is that?
Because the administration is doing everything in its power for the midterms. Every data point is framed to argue against a rate hike. The market is pricing in absolutely zero shock or tail risk. Although the term structure is elevated, it remains within an average range, and the skew is just business as usual.
The market, as I mentioned, is betting on a reflationary melt-up.

The trade is obvious.
First, I already stated last year that I am long long-dated ATM straddles. This has been generating a nice amount of income ever since....
Due to high forward rates, calls are already very expensive, but puts remain cheap, making the underpricing of downside convexity an excellent opportunity. Short 2027-2028 far-OTM calls (or spread) and use the premium to buy the cheap catalyst window that the volatility surface is mispricing (Dec '26 – Jun '27 put spreads).
🙂The risk here is if the shock hits slower, gets delayed, or gets drawn out. In that case, the put spread can be bled dry by theta, and the broker's risk algorithm could ruthlessly liquidate you via the margin requirements on the long-dated short calls before your macro thesis plays out.

Another option is a box spread arbitrage targeting the December-January usurious rate. The risk-free harvesting of this bank balance-sheet premium cleanly covers the theta bleed of my direction-neutral volatility positions.
It is important to continually reiterate that the SPX/yields beta is expected to remain negative, meaning bonds cannot be used as a hedge. This could change, and it is crucial to monitor, but the probability is low. Warsh will be a turning point: he will either confirm or reverse this trend.
Alongside all this, I am long the #oil curve. Due to backwardation, rolling down the curve allows me to continuously pocket a positive roll yield. This leg not only provides protection against the rate-shock scenario, but it constantly pays me to wait, massively improving the survivability of the entire portfolio.
(Remember how I was short backwardation when Iran conflict started? I signaled it here real time, broke it down for you. That trade also printed nicely... now this)
Enjoy.

As I keep saying: 
"Warsh is not priced in,
oil is not priced in,
SPR risk is not priced in,
energy crisis and credit risk is not priced in,
China First Island Chain is not priced in at all.
BoJ is only slightly priced in, market is too complacent about it."
I covered it all in the past months, you just don't listen. I forecasted things ahead of many. Listen to me more carefully... big opportunities there are...

US product stocks started 2026 above every prior year. 

⚠️They just fell below every prior year. 

The yellow line is 2026.

Weeks 1-5: highest stocks in years. 
US refiners well-supplied, cushion above historical norms.

Week 10: Iran war. Hormuz closes. US refiners ramp to maximum utilization to fill global gasoline, diesel and jet fuel gaps left by Middle East outages.

Week 21: stocks crash below every single prior year on the chart.

The cushion that absorbed the first months of the Hormuz shock is now gone.

What's left? refiners already running flat-out, product stocks below historical lows, and hurricane season just starting.

Any further disruption  a Gulf Coast storm, an unscheduled refinery outage, a demand spike hits a system with no inventory buffer and no spare capacity to respond.

This is what "the US is the swing refiner of last resort" looks like when the buffer runs out.

Gasoline and diesel cracks this summer are one bad headline away from spiking hard.

Make it make sense you complete and utter wally.

‼️BREAKING || STARMER BOARDS PUTIN'S OIL TANKER -- AFTER HIS OWN DEFENCE CREW JUMPED SHIP
English Channel -- Royal Marine Commandos have boarded a Russian "shadow fleet" oil tanker in the dark. In front of MoD cameras, the action unfolded like a dream -- fast ropes, helicopters, NCA officers checking cabins, every Navy ship it could muster without snapping in half, and RAF support that looked solid too, unlike a few weeks ago, when the RAF could not catch a stray drone while already on patrol in Romania.

A six-hour raid on the Cameroon-flagged Smyrtos, held off the south coast after sailing from Russia's port near St Petersburg.

On the surface, this was Britain at sea with teeth.

Sir Keir Starmer said the operation dealt "another blow to Russia" and warned those "fuelling Putin's war" that they would not be allowed to hide.

Credit where it's due -- fine deck work by the UK soldiers, sailors and flying men who still know how to do their job on loose change and crossed fingers.

But the real story sat behind the camera.

This raid came after a week in which Starmer's own defence deck split open.

John Healey quit as Defence Secretary, warning that Starmer's defence cash "falls well short" of what Britain needs.

Al Carns quit as Armed Forces Minister, saying the Defence Investment Plan was "neither transformative enough nor sufficiently funded".

So Downing Street got its perfect Sunday picture.

A Russian-linked defenceless tanker with British boots on deck.

And behind it all -- two defence ministers gone because they did not believe the state had put enough money behind the very force it now sent out for the photo call.

The question is whether Westminster has failed them before the next Channel raid, the next Channel drama, and the next Russian tanker that may not sail alone.

What happens when Moscow sends escorts?

What happens when the "shadow fleet" arrives with warships behind it and submarines beneath it?

Will that be the next "historic first" -- or a second Dunkirk with higher resolution press shots in CMYK colours?

Because one boarded tanker does not fill a hollow budget.

One clean operation does not end a defence row.

One shadow fleet ship at anchor off Weymouth does not prove Britain is ready for the threats its own ministers say are coming.

The Smyrtos may now sit under watch off the south coast.

But the larger vessel taking on water is Starmer’s defence policy.

And this week, two of his own men already left the ship.

📊 WHAT MATTERS NEXT WEEK | Events & Catalysts (June 15 – June 19, 2026)
🕒 All times in ET

The upcoming week will be dominated by central banks, consumer spending data, and geopolitics. The spotlight is on Kevin Warsh's first Federal Reserve meeting as Chair, alongside U.S. retail sales and several key global rate decisions. Geopolitics remain a major wildcard after President Trump said over the weekend that a U.S.-Iran agreement could be signed as early as Sunday, which also happens to be his 80th birthday. Iranian officials acknowledged progress but disputed Trump's timeline, suggesting some issues remain unresolved. Weekend indications point to a positive start for risk assets, with Nasdaq 100 futures up 0.5% and WTI crude down 2.3%.

• Highlight of the week: 🇺🇸 Federal Reserve rate decision and Kevin Warsh's first press conference on Wednesday
• Potential Iran deal remains a key driver for oil, inflation expectations, and risk assets
• U.S. retail sales will provide an important read on consumer strength
• Rate decisions from the Fed, BoJ, RBA, SNB, and BoE
• G7 Summit in France with a focus on Iran, trade, and geopolitics
• Accenture, Kroger, Jabil, and CarMax headline the earnings calendar
• HPE Discover and AWS Summit could generate fresh AI and cloud-related headlines
• Triple Witching and S&P index rebalancing may boost market volatility

⸻

📅 Monday, June 15
• G7 Summit begins in France
• Markets watch for developments regarding a potential U.S.-Iran agreement
• HPE Discover Conference kicks off in Las Vegas (4 days)
• Kardigan IPO expected to begin trading
• Earnings: Dave & Buster's ($PLAY)

⸻

📅 Tuesday, June 16
• Earnings: John Wiley & Sons ($WLY), La-Z-Boy ($LZB)
• AWS Summit New York focused on cloud and AI innovation
• SailPoint Investor Day
• Valmont Industries Investor Day
• August WTI crude futures expire
• 8:30 AM 🇺🇸 Housing Starts (May)

⸻

📅 Wednesday, June 17
• Most important trading day of the week
• Earnings: Jabil ($JBL), CarMax ($KMX)
• Ocular Therapeutix Investor Day
• Royal Gold Virtual Roadshow
• 8:30 AM 🇺🇸 Retail Sales MoM (May)
• 8:30 AM 🇺🇸 Core Retail Sales MoM (May)
• 10:30 AM 🇺🇸 Crude Oil Inventories
• 2:00 PM 🇺🇸 Fed Rate Decision
• 2:00 PM 🇺🇸 FOMC Statement
• 2:00 PM 🇺🇸 FOMC Economic Projections (Dot Plot)
• 2:30 PM 🇺🇸 Kevin Warsh Press Conference

⸻

📅 Thursday, June 18
• Earnings: Kroger ($KR), Accenture ($ACN)
• Triple Witching Day with simultaneous expiration of stock options, index options, and futures
• OPEC releases its World Oil Outlook
• FDA decisions expected on therapies from Spero Therapeutics ($SPRO), Merck ($MRK), and Xspray Pharma
• After the close: Quarterly rebalancing of the S&P 500, S&P 400, and S&P 600
• 8:30 AM 🇺🇸 Philadelphia Fed Manufacturing Index (June)
• 8:30 AM 🇺🇸 Initial Jobless Claims

⸻

📅 Friday, June 19
• 🇺🇸 Juneteenth Holiday
• U.S. stock markets closed
• Fed blackout period ends
• 🇨🇳 Dragon Boat Festival
• 🇭🇰 Tuen Ng Festival

⸻

📌 Takeaways
• The Fed meeting is the most important event of the week
• Kevin Warsh's first press conference could reshape rate-cut expectations
• Retail sales will show whether the U.S. consumer remains resilient
• Iran negotiations remain critical for oil prices and market sentiment
• Accenture will provide an important read on enterprise and AI spending trends
• Triple Witching and S&P rebalancing could increase volatility into the weekend

Bottom line: Central banks, geopolitics, and the U.S. consumer will drive markets this week. The Fed, retail sales data, and developments around Iran are likely to determine whether risk appetite continues to improve or investors shift their focus back to macro risks.

The trick here is to subtract costs. What did this conflict cost America? 

1) it did not lesson our standing in the world. Quite the opposite for multiple reasons;

- Iran pissed off all its Arab allies and euro enablers when it struck out against region. Don’t think Arabs haven’t noticed the lack of attacks on Israel itself vs their neighbors.

-American weaponry in full display and primacy. Shredding any countries countermeasures. Autonomous capabilities on display as well, one area of real concern. Worldwide arms purchase agreements between allied powers, Russia and China hurting here with key cancellations.

-geopolitical repositioning. Iran cannot be trusted so new allies and economic countermeasures are taking place in coordination with America; bypassing Iranian waters, UAE exit from OPEC, new Arab pipelines

2) has been a long term economic boon to America and its new geopolitical ally, Venezuela (and soon to be Cuba). 

-like a light was switched VNZ, production is skyrocketing. That oil, instead of paying off Chinese debt at discounted rates, is instead getting market rates and only transiting across the Gulf of America. There was no real regime change in vnz, just an acknowledgment that they are now within geopolitical alignment with Washington. They will profit greatly.

-LNG is a long term investment and commitment. In order to justify the tens of billions in upfront facility costs you must agree to long term 20/30 yr off take contracts. This is locked in. When marginal LNG demand moves to America due to conflict it is many times permanent. And any rational large company and/or nation is forcing itself to consider energy source diversity in their long term planning. America wins that. 

-AMERICAN (including Canada and Latin America) energy production/assets will become even more investable/desirable. Expect more AI and foreign direct investment in o&g assets. Irans inability to strike back will be have a massive psychological effect on investor, opinions of international versus American energy asset.

3) military costs are a major enabler of this contact. A lot of old tech was thrown into Iran to devastate their state and foreign military apparatus. The state is fundamentally unstable, and will be focused on self preservation. Hence the lack of Hamas or hezbollah reprisals. 

-Irans inability to strike back forced them to target allies. This has not been lost on any military in the world. Humiliating performance for Iran and a significant morale and future training/development/culture impact on the us military.

There’s no way here where Iran has accomplished even a few of its goals. It’s humiliated, its foreign apparatus in chaos, distracted in their own country, they’ve been unable to interfere in Syria, which has led them to stabilize. Chinas certainty of a quick win in  Taiwan is shattered if you are VNZ to the mix. 

So at little cost to America Iran has been handicapped Iran regionally, politically, socially and militarily. An absolute schlacking. Oil hardly above $100 and If it was manipulated, that’s even more impressive. Total domination.
